title: Image of God
subtitle: What it means to be human across Scripture
description: The Bible's opening chapter makes a claim about humanity that no other ancient text matches: human beings are created in the image of God. The Hebrew phrase tselem Elohim appears without explanation, as if the writer expected readers to feel its weight without a definition. What does it mean to bear God's image? Genesis offers no systematic answer — instead, it shows image-bearers exercising dominion, cultivating gardens, naming animals, and relating to one another as male and female. The rest of Scripture traces what happens to this image when it is marred by sin, distorted by violence, and ultimately restored in the person of Christ. Paul calls Jesus 'the image of the invisible God,' suggesting that to understand what the image of God looks like, we must look at a specific human life. This journey follows the imago Dei from its first appearance in Genesis through its fracturing, its preservation, its reinterpretation in the Psalms and prophets, and its eschatological restoration — where believers are conformed to the image of the Son.

stops.label: Created in the Image
stops.development: The phrase 'Let us make humanity in our image, according to our likeness' is the climax of the creation narrative. In the ancient Near East, images of gods were placed in temples to represent the deity's authority and presence. Genesis democratizes the concept: every human being — not just kings or priests — is God's image in the world. The immediate context links the image to a vocation: dominion over creation, fruitfulness, and the filling of the earth. The image is not a substance within humans but a relational status — humans represent God to creation and relate to God as no other creature can.
stops.what_changes: Humanity is established as God's representative in creation. The image is functional and relational, not a metaphysical substance — it defines what humans are for, not just what they are.
stops.bridge_to_next: The image is announced in the cosmic poetry of Genesis 1. But the second creation account slows down to show what image-bearing looks like up close — dust and breath, garden and task, solitude and partnership.

stops.label: Dust and Breath
stops.development: God forms the human from the dust of the ground and breathes into his nostrils the breath of life. The image of God is not spirit trapped in matter but the whole person — dust animated by divine breath. The Hebrew adam comes from adamah (ground), embedding the human in the earth even as divine breath elevates him beyond it. This dual nature — earthy and God-breathed — defines the human condition throughout Scripture. We are not angels temporarily imprisoned in bodies; we are embodied creatures whose physical existence is part of the image, not a distortion of it.
stops.what_changes: The image of God is embodied. Human dignity includes the body, not despite it. This grounds the biblical insistence on bodily resurrection and the goodness of material creation.
stops.bridge_to_next: The dust-and-breath creature walked with God in the garden — until a choice fractured the relationship. The fall did not erase the image, but it damaged the mirror. From Genesis 3 onward, the question is whether the image can be repaired.

stops.label: The Image Marred
stops.development: After eating the fruit, the humans' first act is to cover themselves — hiding from each other and from God. The image is not destroyed but distorted. Where they had represented God's authority with confidence, they now exercise it through blame, manipulation, and eventually violence. Genesis 3 does not use the phrase 'image of God,' but the narrative shows the image cracking: the relationship with God becomes fearful, the relationship between man and woman becomes competitive, and the relationship with creation becomes adversarial. The representational function remains — humans still act in the world — but what they represent has become ambiguous.
stops.what_changes: Sin distorts but does not erase the image. Humans still bear God's likeness, but their representation of God is now fractured — marked by shame, fear, and the impulse to hide.
stops.bridge_to_next: The distortion deepened rapidly. Cain murdered Abel, and by Genesis 6 violence filled the earth. After the flood, God reaffirmed the image in a surprising context — a law against murder that grounded human dignity in the very thing humanity had nearly forfeited.

stops.label: The Image Reaffirmed
stops.development: 'Whoever sheds the blood of a human, by a human shall that person's blood be shed, for in the image of God has God made humanity.' This is the first reappearance of 'image of God' since Genesis 1, and it comes in a post-flood world scarred by violence. The logic is striking: murder is wrong not because of social contract or utilitarian calculation but because the victim bears God's image. Even after the fall, even after the flood, every human being retains this status. The Noahic covenant makes the image of God the foundation of justice — a principle that James will later extend to the ethics of speech: 'With the tongue we curse human beings, who have been made in God's likeness.'
stops.what_changes: The image survives the fall and the flood. It becomes the basis for ethics — not an abstract theological category but the ground of concrete obligations toward every human being.
stops.bridge_to_next: Genesis established the image; the wisdom literature reflected on it. In Psalm 8, David looked at the night sky and asked the question that every thoughtful person eventually asks: given the vastness of creation, what are human beings that God should care about them?

stops.label: A Little Lower than God
stops.development: Psalm 8 is a meditation on Genesis 1 in the key of wonder. The psalmist gazes at the heavens — moon and stars set in place by God's fingers — and marvels that the God who made all this is 'mindful' of mortal humans. The Hebrew me'at describes humans as 'a little lower than elohim' — a word that can mean God or heavenly beings. The psalm reaffirms the image by restating the dominion mandate in poetic form: all things under human feet, flocks and herds, birds and fish. But the psalm's tone is not triumphalist; it begins and ends with 'O Lord, our Lord, how majestic is your name in all the earth.' Human dignity is always derivative — it flows from the God whose image we bear, not from any inherent self-sufficiency.
stops.what_changes: The image is reframed in the language of worship. Human dignity is not a claim humans make for themselves but a gift they receive with wonder — and it is always understood in relation to God's greater majesty.
stops.bridge_to_next: The Psalms celebrated human dignity; the prophets confronted its abuse. Isaiah's vision of the suffering servant introduced a paradox: the one who most fully bears God's image would be so disfigured that people would look away. The image of God would be restored through its apparent destruction.

stops.label: The True Image
stops.development: Paul calls Christ 'the image of the invisible God, the firstborn over all creation.' Where Adam bore the image imperfectly and transmitted it in damaged form, Jesus is the image without distortion — the human being God always intended. The Colossians hymn places Christ as both the agent of creation ('by him all things were created') and the goal of redemption ('through him to reconcile to himself all things'). In Jesus, the image of God is not merely restored to its Genesis 1 condition but revealed in its fullest expression. He exercises the dominion Adam was given — not by ruling with force but by serving unto death. The image of God, it turns out, looks like a cross.
stops.what_changes: The image of God is no longer an abstract concept but a visible person. Christ reveals what the image was always meant to be — and it looks nothing like the power, self-sufficiency, or autonomy that fallen humanity expected.
stops.bridge_to_next: If Christ is the true image, what happens to those who follow him? Paul's answer was not that believers admire the image from a distance but that they are being transformed into it — a process that begins now and reaches completion in resurrection.

stops.label: Conformed to the Image
stops.development: Paul writes that God predestined believers 'to be conformed to the image of his Son, so that he might be the firstborn among many brothers and sisters.' The image of God, damaged in Adam, is being restored in Christ — not by returning to Eden but by advancing toward something Eden only anticipated. The Greek summorphos suggests a sharing of form, an ongoing transformation in which believers progressively reflect Christ's character. This is not instantaneous but eschatological — Paul elsewhere describes it as moving 'from glory to glory.' The church, collectively, becomes the community of the restored image, a humanity being remade in the likeness of the one who is the exact representation of God.
stops.what_changes: The image of God becomes eschatological — not just a past endowment but a future destination. Believers are not merely recovering what Adam lost but being drawn toward a glory Adam never attained.
stops.bridge_to_next: The transformation Paul described is both already and not yet. Ephesians draws it into the present tense — a 'new self' that believers are called to put on now, even as they await the final renewal.

stops.label: The New Self
stops.development: Paul instructs believers to 'put off the old self, which is being corrupted by its deceitful desires, and to put on the new self, created to be like God in true righteousness and holiness.' The language of creation echoes Genesis — the new self is 'created' (ktisthenta) in God's likeness, as if Paul is describing a second creation. The ethical imperatives that follow — truthfulness, controlled anger, honest work, gracious speech — are not arbitrary moral rules but descriptions of what the restored image looks like in daily life. To bear God's image is not mystical abstraction but concrete practice: the way we speak, work, forgive, and relate to one another either reflects or obscures the God whose likeness we carry.
stops.what_changes: The image of God reaches its practical expression. Bearing God's image is not a status to contemplate but a life to live — manifested in the ordinary ethics of truthful speech, honest labor, and generous community.
